items.title: Strong global radiative effects from wildfire dark brown carbon
items.body:
Download PDF

Article
    
        
            Open access

Published: 12 May 2026

Strong global radiative effects from wildfire dark brown carbon
                    Lulu Xu 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-4411-84761, Guangxing Lin 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-5443-39152, Chenglai Wu 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-8397-24241, Xi Chen 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-9952-90481, Hao Wang3, Cheng Chen 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-7768-95424, Rajan K. Chakrabarty 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0001-5753-99375, Meng Gao 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-7323-16306, Ville Vakkari7,8, Pieter G. van Zyl8 & …Xiaohong Liu 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-3994-59559 Show authors

Nature Geoscience

(2026) Cite this article

Subjects
        
            Atmospheric scienceClimate change

AbstractWildfires emit large quantities of brown carbon, a class of light-absorbing organic aerosols whose climate effects remain poorly constrained. Brown carbon displays a broad spectrum of absorptivity, ranging from weakly absorbing chromophores in the near-ultraviolet to strongly absorbing species that extend into the visible spectrum—yet its optical properties, global distribution and radiative influence remain largely uncertain. Here we assess the global prevalence and optical characteristics of dark brown carbon through an integrated analysis of aircraft measurements, ground-based observations and satellite retrievals. We show that this strongly absorbing aerosol is widespread in wildfire plumes around the world. Measured dark brown carbon mass absorption efficiencies range from 0.5 to 1.5 m2 g−1 at 500 nm, with absorption frequently comparable to—or even exceeding—that of black carbon. When these observationally constrained optical properties are incorporated into a global aerosol–climate model, we estimate a direct radiative effect of +0.097 W m−2 (spanning +0.050 to +0.276 W m−2) attributable to wildfire-derived brown carbon, with the upper bound surpassing black carbon’s contribution and extending into mid- and high-latitude regions, including the Arctic. These results position dark brown carbon as a critical yet underrecognized contributor to wildfire radiative forcing, highlighting the need to account for its substantial warming influence in climate assessments.

MainGlobal warming has intensified wildfire activities worldwide, with both frequency and severity rising1,2,3. Wildfires emit vast quantities of atmospheric particles, including black carbon (BC) and organic aerosols, which influence Earth’s climate by absorbing and scattering solar radiation, modifying cloud microphysics and altering atmospheric circulation patterns4,5,6.While the light-absorbing properties of BC are relatively well understood, brown carbon (BrC), a light-absorbing component of organic aerosols, remains poorly constrained. BrC is chemically and physically more complex than BC, with diverse sources, formation pathways and optical properties. It can be directly emitted during biomass combustion or secondarily formed through atmospheric reactions7,8,9. Once in the atmosphere, BrC undergoes photochemical ageing and bleaching, leading to dynamic changes in its absorptive characteristics10,11. The absorptivity of BrC can vary by up to two orders of magnitude depending on its origin and evolution, contributing to considerable uncertainty in climate assessments12.Traditionally, BC has been considered the dominant absorber in wildfire plumes13,14. However, BrC exhibits a wide spectrum of absorptivity, ranging from weakly absorbing chromophores that primarily absorb in the near-ultraviolet range (300–400 nm) (refs. 15,16) to strongly absorbing species with substantial absorption extending well beyond 500 nm into the visible spectrum17,18,19,20,21. Such strongly absorbing BrC has been documented in diverse combustion sources, including East Asian outflow17, laboratory biomass burning emissions8,20,21, laboratory generated tar balls18 and marine engine exhaust19. Recent studies have termed this strongly absorbing end as ‘dark BrC’ (d-BrC)22, which consists of larger molecular weight, multi-ring polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons with extensive conjugated electron systems12, in contrast to smaller structures absorbing primarily at shorter wavelengths15,16. d-BrC was found to even surpass BC in the total aerosol absorption from wildfire events in the western USA22, challenging the traditional assumption of BC dominance in wildfire plume absorption. However, whether d-BrC is a localized phenomenon specific to certain combustion conditions or represents a systematic global feature of wildfire emissions remains unresolved.Along with advances in measurements, modelling of BrC’s radiative effect has progressed in multiple studies23,24,25,26,27,28,29. Models incorporating only weakly absorbing BrC underestimate absorption at longer wavelengths25,26. Other models have included strongly absorbing d-BrC, using field-observed or laboratory-derived optical properties23,24,27,28,29, accounting for enhanced absorption in global climate simulations. However, critical gaps remain in our understanding of BrC’s global radiative impact. First, existing models rely on BrC optical properties from either localized field observations, which cannot capture the diversity of wildfire emissions globally, or laboratory experiments, which may not represent ambient atmospheric conditions. Second, inconsistent optical property parameterizations across previous studies have led to large inter-model spreads in estimated BrC direct radiative effect (that is, perturbation to Earth’s energy balance by absorbing solar radiation), ranging from +0.03 W m−2 to +0.57 W m−2 (refs. 25,26,28,30). This uncertainty hinders robust climate policy formulation.In this Article, we conduct comprehensive analyses of global wildfire observations, including airborne measurements, ground-based remote sensing, in situ sampling and satellite retrievals, to characterize the spectral absorption properties of BrC. By applying advanced apportionment techniques, we quantify BrC contributions to shortwave absorption and identify widespread d-BrC occurrences across diverse ecosystems. Incorporating these refined BrC properties into a global aerosol–climate model, we provide updated estimates of wildfire BrC’s direct radiative effect (DRE). Our findings reveal ubiquitous d-BrC presence in wildfire emissions worldwide, challenging BC’s prevailing dominance and highlighting BrC’s under-appreciated role in Earth’s energy budget.Ubiquity of dark BrC in wildfiresWe first analyse aerosol absorption measurements from multiple aircraft field campaigns and ground station observations (Supplementary Fig. 1) by isolating the absorption contributions of BrC and BC. This apportionment is performed using the measured mass concentrations and corresponding mass absorption efficiency (MAE) values of BC, allowing us to estimate the BrC absorption as the difference (Methods). Figure 1a–e presents the wavelength-dependent absorption coefficients (babs) of BrC and BC for the wildfire events observed during aircraft field campaigns in five geographic regions.Fig. 1: Absorption coefficients and mass proportions of BrC and BC during wildfire events.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imagea, Absorption coefficients (Mm−1, inverse megametres) of wildfire-derived BrC and BC obtained from DC3 aircraft observations (n = 690). b, From SEAC4RS aircraft observations (n = 27). c, From FIREX-AQ aircraft observations (n = 125,115). d, From GoAmazon ground station observations (n = 105). e, From ORACLES aircraft observations (n = 2,862). f, Mass proportion of BC in total carbon components (BC + OC) during wildfire events obtained from these five aircraft campaigns. The reported n values represent the total number of data points used to calculate the statistics for each bar, derived from independent observational samples across parameter configurations in the absorption apportionment method (Methods). Data are presented as mean values. Error bars represent the minimum–maximum range of absorption coefficients derived from parameter sensitivity analysis in the apportionment method.Source dataAcross all five regions, wildfire-derived BrC exhibits substantial absorption not only in the near-ultraviolet and short-wavelength visible range (~400 nm), but also in the mid- to long-visible wavelengths (500–700 nm). This relatively weak spectral dependence of absorption contrasts sharply with the conventional behaviour of weakly absorbing BrC, which typically shows strong absorption near 400 nm but negligible absorption beyond 500 nm (refs. 15,16). The observed spectral pattern indicates the presence of ‘d-BrC’, a class of BrC with enhanced absorption throughout the visible spectrum.Notably, in the USA wildfire events sampled during the three field campaigns: the Deep Convective Clouds and Chemistry Project (DC3), the Studies of Emissions, Atmospheric Composition, Clouds and Climate Coupling by Regional Surveys (SEAC4RS) and the Fire Influence on Regional to Global Environments and Air Quality (FIREX-AQ), BrC absorption is comparable to or even greater than that of BC across much of the visible range. By contrast, in the wildfire events sampled during the Observations and Modelling of the Green Ocean Amazon (GoAmazon) campaign in the Amazon and the ObseRvations of Aerosols above CLouds and their intEractionS (ORACLES) campaign in southern Africa, BrC absorption is consistently lower than the BC absorption. These regional differences can be explained by variations in their carbonaceous aerosol composition (Fig. 1f). In US wildfire plumes, organic aerosols dominate the particulate matter mass, with BC contributing less than 3%, thereby allowing BrC to play a more dominant role in the light absorption. In contrast, the Amazon and southern African wildfire plumes have higher BC mass fractions (10–20%), resulting in stronger relative contributions from BC to the total absorption. These differences in BC mass fractions are primarily attributed to variations in fuel types, moisture content and fire management practices across different regions. US wildfires predominantly burn temperate forest fuels that typically maintain higher moisture content31, which promotes smouldering-dominated combustion that favours organic aerosol formation over BC production32,33. In contrast, fires in the Amazon and southern Africa often involve drier grassland, savannah and agricultural residue burning with more flaming combustion phases that promote higher BC emissions factors34.To further confirm the presence of d-BrC, we calculate the mass absorption efficiency of organic carbon (MAEOC) based on observationally derived BrC absorption coefficients and co-located carbonaceous aerosol mass measurements (Fig. 2a–e). While direct BrC mass measurements are not available, MAEOC provides a reasonable proxy for characterizing the BrC absorptivity. In all cases, MAEOC peaks at 404 nm, with values ranging from 0.5 to 3.5 m2 g−1. The highest value is observed during the ORACLES campaign (3.21 m2 g−1). Remarkably, at 500 nm, a wavelength where weakly absorbing BrC typically exhibits MAEOC < 0.1 m2 g−1 (refs. 15,35,36), several campaigns, including SEAC4RS and ORACLES, still show MAEOC values > 1.0 m2 g−1. These values are consistent with the spectral properties of d-BrC reported in recent studies22, where d-BrC exhibited MAE values around 1.0 m2 g−1 at 500 nm.Fig. 2: Variations in MAEOC with wavelength during wildfire events.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imagea–e, MAEOC values at different wavelengths obtained from DC3 (a), SEAC4RS (b), FIREX-AQ (c), GoAmazon (d) and ORACLES (e) campaigns, respectively. f, Comparison of absorption Ångström exponents of organic carbon (AAEOC) of wildfire smokes during these five campaigns. Data are presented as mean values. The shaded regions in a–e represent the minimum–maximum range of MAEOC derived from parameter sensitivity analysis in the separation method, while the error bars in f represent the minimum–maximum range of AAEOC derived from parameter sensitivity analysis in the apportionment method. The numbers of data points used in f correspond to those reported in Fig. 1 (n = 690; 27; 125,115; 105 and 2,862 for DC3, SEAC4RS, FIREX-AQ, GoAmazon and ORACLES, respectively), derived from independent observational samples across parameter configurations in the apportionment method.Source dataThe spectral dependence of aerosol absorption is further characterized using the absorption Ångström exponent (AAE), which quantifies how the absorption changes with wavelength. Figure 2f shows AAE values of OC (AAEOC) for the wildfire events observed in the five campaigns. The AAEOC values span a range of 1.3 to 3.8, with an average of 2.7 across all campaigns. These values are indicative of substantial absorption across the visible spectrum, reinforcing the conclusion that d-BrC is prevalent in wildfire plumes across these diverse regions. The derived AAEOC values are slightly higher than the 1.5–2.0 range for pure d-BrC reported by Chakrabarty et al.22, reflecting the mixture of strongly and weakly absorbing BrC in ambient wildfire plumes analysed in our study. Notably, they are considerably lower than those typically reported for weakly absorbing BrC, which generally range from 4 to 11 in literature37,38,39, further supporting the distinct spectral characteristics of d-BrC.Contribution of BrC to wildfire aerosol absorptionBuilding on the findings from synchronized optical and carbon component measurements, we extend our analysis to assess the BrC absorption in global wildfire events using ground-based and satellite remote sensing data. Here we estimate the BrC absorption contribution by separating it from total aerosol absorption measured at broader spatial and temporal scales.Figure 3a–g shows the derived aerosol absorption optical depths (AAOD) of BrC and BC at multiple AERONET sites affected by wildfires, including regions in North America, northern Asia, South America and Southeast Asia. Due to limited AERONET coverage during fire seasons in Africa, we additionally include observations from the Welgegund ground station in southern Africa (Fig. 3h).Fig. 3: Comparison of absorption properties between BrC and BC in global wildfire events.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imagea–g, AAOD of OC and BC from wildfire sources at monitoring stations in Monterey (a), NASA Ames (b), CUIABA-MIRANDA (c), Yakutsk (d), Chiang Dao (e), Chiang Mai Met Sta (f) and Luang Namtha (g), all of which are from the AERONET observation network. h, Absorption coefficients of OC and BC from wildfire sources at the Welgegund ground station. Data are presented as mean values. Error bars represent the minimum–maximum range of AAOD and absorption coefficients derived from parameter sensitivity analysis in the apportionment method. The numbers of data points (n) for a–h are 15, 27, 15, 36, 78, 39, 81 and 183, respectively, derived from independent observational samples across parameter configurations in the apportionment method.Source dataThese observations reveal that wildfire-derived BrC absorption is not confined to near-ultraviolet wavelengths but extends into the visible spectrum. At 404 nm, BrC AAOD approaches that of BC or even exceeds it at most sites, including CUIABA, Yakutsk, Chiang Dao, Chiang Mai, Luang Namtha and Welgegund. Furthermore, BrC maintains appreciable absorption at 532 nm and 660 nm (wavelengths where weakly absorbing BrC is expected to have much less influence), suggesting the presence of d-BrC across diverse wildfire regions. At Yakutsk, Luang Namtha and Welgegund, BrC absorption at mid- to long-visible wavelengths (that is, 550 nm and 660 nm) still accounts for more than half of BC absorption, indicating that BrC substantially contributes to total particulate light absorption in wildfire plumes at these sites. Notably, the results from Welgegund present a different OC absorption ratio compared to ORACLES observations, despite both campaigns being conducted in the South African region. This discrepancy can be attributed to the higher NOx concentrations at Welgegund due to industrial sources within a few hundred kilometres of the site40. These higher NOx concentrations could notably enhance BrC formation processes41.To complement the ground-based analysis and expand the spatial-temporal coverage, we also examine wildfire-derived BrC and BC AAOD using global satellite retrieval products. Supplementary Fig. 2 and Fig. 4 present comparisons of BrC and BC AAOD at 404 nm and 532 nm, respectively, for major wildfire-prone regions during October 2012. Across all wildfire source regions, BrC AAOD either equals or exceeds BC AAOD, reaffirming BrC’s role in the wildfire aerosol absorption. Notably, the dominance of BrC is evident not only at 404 nm but also at 532 nm, confirming its substantial influence across the mid-visible spectrum.Fig. 4: Comparison of AAOD for carbon components BC and OC from satellite data.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imagea, Global distribution of AAOD of BC (AAODBC) at 532 nm derived from POLDER satellite retrieval in October 2012. b, Global distribution of AAOD of OC (AAODOC) at 532 nm. c, Comparison of mean values of AAODBC and AAODOC across the globe and within major wildfire source regions. The latitude and longitude ranges of the five major wildfire source regions are as follows: North America (18° N–70° N, 188° E–306° E), Northern Asia (30° N–75° N, 60° E–180° E), Africa (30° S–15° N, 0°–60° E), South America (30° S–0°, 280° E–320° E) and Southeastern Asia (10° S–30° N, 90° E–130° E). Basemap from Natural Earth (https://www.naturalearthdata.com).Source dataTo evaluate the seasonal importance of BrC, we perform similar analyses for January, April and July (Supplementary Figs. 3–8). In all cases, the results consistently show strong contributions from BrC to total aerosol absorption, further validating the global and year-round presence of wildfire d-BrC.Together, these multi-platform observational datasets establish that BrC plays a globally substantial role in the absorption of solar radiation by wildfire aerosols. This widespread influence has previously been underestimated in current climate assessments and underscores the need to reevaluate the radiative effect of wildfire aerosols with improved representation of BrC optical properties.Radiative effects from wildfire BrCTo quantify the radiative effect of wildfire-derived BrC, we used a global aerosol–climate model: NCAR’s Community Earth System Model (CESM) and modified the optical properties of primary organic aerosol from biomass burning emissions by using MAEOC values derived from our observational analyses (Methods). To capture variability of MAEOC across diverse atmospheric conditions, we conducted sensitivity simulations using the lower and upper bounds of derived MAEOC from observational analyses (DC3, SEAC4RS, FIREX-AQ, GoAmazon and ORACLES campaigns).We first evaluated the modelled mass and light absorption of BC and OC by comparing the simulations against all observations from wildfire events (Supplementary Figs. 9–13). Compared to observations, the simulated BC and OC mass concentrations are substantially underestimated (Supplementary Fig. 9), probably due to model underestimation in the biomass burning aerosol emissions42,43,44. However, the BC/OC mass ratios in most campaigns show reasonable agreement between simulations and observations (Supplementary Fig. 10). The modelled total absorption coefficient at 532 nm is also underestimated across most campaigns. We further compared the contribution of OC to total aerosol absorption at 532 nm, which reflects whether the simulated absorbing fraction of OC is accurately represented. By incorporating BrC absorption in the model, particularly with MAEOC values reflecting the moderate absorption intensity (BRC_BASE case), the simulated OC absorption contributions align closely with observations across all campaigns (Supplementary Figs. 12 and 13). One notable exception is the ORACLES campaign, where the OC absorption contribution is overestimated, probably due to the model underestimation of BC mass ratio for this campaign (Supplementary Fig. 10). Figure 5 shows the top-of-atmosphere (TOA) DRE attributable to BrC and BC absorption from wildfire emissions. Globally, the BrC-induced DRE in the BRC_BASE case (Model simulations), representing moderate BrC absorption, is +0.097 W m−2. The sensitivity simulations yield a range of +0.050 W m−2 in the BRC_MIN case (with the weakest BrC absorption) to +0.276 W m−2 in the BRC_MAX case (with the strongest BrC absorption). The values in the BRC_BASE and BRC_MAX cases are lower than those in Wang et al.29, who reported a global mean absorption DRE of +0.208 W m−2 (+0.02 to +0.68 W m−2) for BrC from biomass burning. Several factors contribute to this difference. First, our simulations employ optical properties of BrC derived from global observational campaigns, whereas Wang et al.29 utilized measurements from Chakrabarty et al.22, which primarily represent conditions over North America. Second, Wang et al.29 incorporated the enhancement effect on the BrC absorption due to oxidation by NO3 radical at nighttime in their simulations, an effect that may not be universally applicable to wildfire-derived BrC across all burning conditions. Note that our uncertainty range is narrower as we did not consider uncertainties in biomass burning aerosol emissions, which contribute to the broader range in Wang et al.29. In the BRC_MAX case, BrC-induced warming surpasses that of BC ( + 0.163 W m−2), revealing that BrC from wildfires may be a more important driver of solar absorption than previously recognized.Fig. 5: Global distribution of DRE at the TOA caused by wildfire-derived BrC and BC absorption.The alternative text for this image may have been generated using AI.Full size imagea, BrC absorption radiative effect at TOA in the model experiment considering moderate BrC absorption (BRC_BASE). b, BrC absorption radiative effect at TOA in the simulation with weakest BrC absorption (BRC_MIN). c, BrC absorption radiative effect at TOA in the simulation with strongest BrC absorption (BRC_MAX). d, BC absorption radiative effect at TOA. The values in the boxes indicate the global average DRE. Basemap from Natural Earth (https://www.naturalearthdata.com).Source dataFurthermore, the spatial distribution of BrC-induced radiative heating shows extensive warming over high-latitude regions in the Northern Hemisphere, an area highly sensitive to radiative perturbations due to surface albedo feedbacks. As shown in Supplementary Fig. 14, the substantial emissions and burden of OC in the Arctic regions are the primary drivers of this pronounced heating. In the BRC_MAX case, whereas BrC-induced heating is comparable to BC in mid- and low-latitude regions, BrC exhibits greater spatial extent and magnitude of warming than those of BC in the Arctic regions. This suggests that wildfire-derived BrC may have amplified impacts on the Arctic cryosphere and climate. These findings indicate that BrC may play a more important role than previously recognized in the Arctic climate change, highlighting the need to improve current representations of BrC in global climate models.Global climate significance of wildfire BrCWildfires emit large quantities of BrC aerosols with variable optical properties whose global radiative effects remain poorly understood. Through comprehensive analyses of multi-platform observational datasets, including aircraft, ground-based and satellite measurements, we show the widespread presence of strongly light-absorbing ‘d-BrC’ in wildfire plumes. These aerosols exhibit substantial absorption across the visible spectrum, with mass absorption efficiencies (MAEOC) between 0.5 and 1.5 m2 g−1 at ~500 nm. In several wildfire events, BrC absorption at visible wavelengths equals or even surpasses that of BC, highlighting BrC as a substantial absorber.Using a global aerosol–climate model with observationally constrained optical properties, we find that wildfire-derived BrC exerts a DRE of +0.097 W m−2 (range: +0.050 to +0.276 W m−2). With strongest-absorbing d-BrC, the atmospheric warming of wildfire BrC exceeds that of wildfire BC ( + 0.163 W m−2). Our estimate of BrC’s DRE may be biased low due to the model underestimation in biomass burning emissions, though the relative importance versus BC remains robust.A key advance of this study is establishing the global prevalence of d-BrC through multi-platform observations spanning diverse conditions. While previous studies identified d-BrC primarily from laboratory or from localized observations, we provide near-global evidence that d-BrC is widespread in real-world biomass burning aerosols. By constraining a global model with OC optical properties derived from this globally representative dataset, we provide estimates of BrC radiative effects that capture the integrated absorption characteristics across diverse real-world conditions. This approach reduces uncertainties in global wildfire climate impact. Model limitations and uncertainties are discussed in Supplementary Materials 2 and 3.The climatic significance of wildfire-derived BrC extends beyond its radiative forcing magnitude. Unlike BC with peak emissions in tropical regions, OC demonstrates substantial emissions and burdens across Northern Hemisphere high-latitude regions (Supplementary Fig. 14). However, our Arctic estimates are uncertain. Our simulations assume spatially uniform BrC optical properties based on constraints from mid- and low-latitude regions, as targeted Arctic BrC measurements are lacking. Future observational campaigns in boreal and Arctic regions are critically needed to directly constrain BrC properties in these climatically sensitive areas.In summary, wildfire-derived BrC, especially d-BrC, is an important and underrecognized contributor to global atmospheric heating. Future climate projections should explicitly incorporate d-BrC in addition to weakly absorbing BrC to accurately assess the role of wildfire aerosols in Earth’s climate system.MethodsObservational dataTo explore the absorption properties of wildfire-derived BrC and its contribution to total aerosol absorption, we collected and analysed wildfire-derived aerosol observational data covering global regions, including observations from aircraft campaigns (DC3, SEAC4RS, FIREX-AQ and ORACLES), ground stations (GoAmazon and Welgegund), ground-based remote sensing network (AERONET) and satellite retrievals (POLDER/GRASP). Detailed information on observational datasets can be found in Supplementary Fig. 1 and Supplementary Table 1.Among these observational data, five field campaigns—DC3 (conducted over Colorado, Oklahoma–Texas and Alabama during May–June 2012)45, SEAC4RS (southeastern United States during August–September 2013)46, FIREX-AQ (western United States during July–September 2019)47, GoAmazon (Manaus, Brazil during September–October 2014)48 and ORACLES (southern Africa during August–September 2016)49—can simultaneously provide total aerosol absorption measurements at multiple wavelengths and carbonaceous aerosol mass information. These campaigns employed sophisticated airborne instrumentation including photoacoustic spectrometers (PAS), single-particle soot photometers (SP2) and aerosol mass spectrometers (AMS) to measure light absorption at three wavelengths (ranging from 404 to 660 nm depending on the campaign) along with BC and organic aerosol (OA) mass concentrations. Through these measurements, we can not only quantify the contribution of wildfire-derived BrC to total aerosol absorption but also derive the key optical properties of wildfire-derived BrC.Complementary data from the AERONET global network of ground-based sun photometers (operating at eight sites from 2018 to 2021)50,51,52, multi-wavelength in situ aerosol absorption measurements from the Welgegund atmospheric monitoring station in South Africa53 and POLDER/GRASP satellite retrievals (providing global coverage for 2012)54,55 supply additional aerosol optical property information. The Welgegund absorption data is obtained from a 7-wavelength Aethalometer AE-31, which is corrected according to Collaud Coen et al.56 based on comparison with a co-located Multi-Angle Absorption Photometer at Welgegund. While these datasets do not provide carbonaceous aerosol mass concentration measurements, they offer valuable geographical and temporal coverages that we use to supplement our analyses of BrC absorption around the globe and validate our findings across different scales and environments.Isolating biomass burning data pointsTo obtain the absorption properties of wildfire-derived BrC and its contribution to the total aerosol absorption, we applied different screening approaches tailored to the available measurements from each observation platform.Aircraft and ground in situ measurementsFollowing the methodology of Brown et al.57, we applied a dual-threshold screening approach to isolate data points dominated by biomass burning emissions while excluding interference from dust aerosols and other combustion sources: (1) BC mass concentration threshold: for aircraft observation samples (DC3, SEAC4RS, FIREX-AQ, ORACLES), we used BC > 0.9 µg m−3; for ground station observation samples (GoAmazon), we used BC > 0.3 µg m−3. The lower threshold for ground observations accounts for lower-background BC concentrations and reduced dilution effects near the surface compared to elevated plumes sampled by aircraft. (2) Aerosol composition threshold: on the basis of multi-model simulation results from Brown et al.57, regions dominated by biomass burning are characterized by BC and OA comprising more than 85% of the total aerosol mass. We therefore required that BC and OA from biomass burning sources comprised more than 85% of the total submicron aerosol mass:$$\frac{\mathrm{OA}+\mathrm{BC}}{\mathrm{Total\_Aerosol}}\ge 0.85$$
                    (1)
                $$\mathrm{Total\_Aerosol}=\mathrm{OA}+\,\mathrm{BC}+\mathrm{Tot\_Inorg}$$
                    (2)
                where Tot_Inorg = ammonium (NH4 +) + sulfate (SO4 2−) + chloride (Cl−) + nitrate (NO3 −). BC was measured by SP2 (Single Particle Soot Photometer), a laser-induced incandescence instrument for measuring refractory BC (rBC) mass content of individual accumulation-mode aerosol particles. OA and inorganic salts (Tot_Inorg) were measured by AMS (Aerosol Mass Spectrometer). All measurements were limited to aerosol particles with diameters < 1 μm (PM1), which effectively excludes the majority of sea salt and dust aerosol masses that are predominantly found in the coarse mode (> 1 μm). The combination of both criteria, applied to submicron aerosols where biomass burning emissions dominate, effectively distinguishes biomass burning aerosols from other anthropogenic combustion sources.Welgegund station dataDue to the lack of BC and OA aerosol mass concentration observations, we selected the typical biomass burning season (August–September) as the analysis period based on regional fire activity patterns.AERONET ground-based dataFor AERONET observations, we utilized the biomass burning period identification from Tanada et al.52, who identified intense fire periods at seven stations in wildfire-prone areas between 2018 and 2021 (Supplementary Table 4) based on aerosol optical property products including aerosol optical depth (AOD), single scattering albedo and Ångström exponent (AE). To exclude dust contamination from the selected biomass burning periods, we applied the criteria of AAE > 1.0 and AE < 1.5 following Cazorla et al.58 and removed data points that meet the criteria.POLDER/GRASP satellite retrievalsFor POLDER/GRASP satellite retrievals, biomass burning periods were identified based on the GFED (Global Fire Emissions Database) global wildfire carbon emission inventory. Similar to AERONET data, dust contamination was excluded by applying the criteria of AAE > 1.0 and AE < 1.5 following Cazorla et al.58, and data points that meet the criteria were removed.Deriving BrC light absorptionAfter completing data screening, we further separated the absorption of BrC and BC from the observed total aerosol absorption data. Depending on the availability of observational data, we employed two different separation methods.Combined mass-optical approachFor processes with synchronous observations of total aerosol absorption and carbonaceous aerosol concentrations, including DC3, SEAC4RS, FIREX-AQ, GoAmazon and ORACLES, we referenced the separation method in Chakrabarty et al.22 to derive the absorption of OC (BrC) and BC:$${b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{BC}}(550)={{\mathrm{Mass}}_{\mathrm{BC}}\times \mathrm{MAE}}_{\mathrm{BC},\,550}$$
                    (3)
                $${b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{BC}}(\lambda )={b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{BC}}(550)\times ({\lambda /550)}^{-{\mathrm{AAE}}_{\mathrm{BC}}}$$
                    (4)
                $${b}_{{\rm{abs}},{\rm{OC}}}(\lambda )={b}_{{\rm{abs}}}(\lambda )-{b}_{{\rm{abs}},{\rm{BC}}}(\lambda )$$
                    (5)
                where MAEBC,550 is the mass absorption efficiency of BC at 550 nm; MassBC is the mass concentration of BC; AAEBC is the absorption Ångström exponent of BC; babs, BC(λ) is the light absorption coefficient of BC at wavelength λ; babs(λ) is the total light absorption of aerosol at wavelength λ; and babs, OC(λ) is the light absorption coefficient of OC at wavelength λ.Normally, measured MAE values for freshly generated BC at 550 nm were 7.5 m2 g−1 (refs. 13,59). The co-emission of BC and organic matters from biomass burning can additionally lead to internal mixing, which can enhance BC absorption by serving as a radiation lens60. Given that ambient BC particles are typically aged and coated during atmospheric transport, we applied an absorption enhancement factor to account for the presence of coatings on ambient aged BC60,61. Following Beerler et al.62, who reported a mean enhancement factor of 1.5 for BC in wildfire plumes, we adopted this value for our analysis, yielding a most likely MAEBC of 11.25 m2 g−1 (7.5 m2 g−1 × 1.5) for ambient BC at 550 nm. Additionally, we considered the broader atmospheric variability of enhancement factors ranging from 1.0 to 1.8, accounting for variations in particle size and mixing state60,61,63,64. This range corresponds to MAEBC values of 7.5 ~ 13.5 m2 g−1 for fresh to heavily coated BC in the atmosphere.The absorption Ångström exponent of BC (AAEBC) is difficult to derive directly from field observations and is typically obtained through numerical calculations65,66,67,68,69. Following Chakrabarty and Heinson68, who applied the dipole–dipole approximation electromagnetic theory to simulate fractal BC aggregates with varying degrees of coating, we adopted AAEBC of 1.0 for our most likely value. Their simulated BC morphologies—including bare aggregates with point-contacting monomers, partially coated aggregates and heavily embedded aggregates—are representative of typical wildfire BC particles70, making their findings particularly applicable to our study. Their calculations demonstrated that MAEBC maintains an inverse wavelength dependency (λ−1) regardless of coating amount, which supports the use of AAEBC = 1.0 across the visible spectrum for both bare and coated BC aggregates. Additionally, we considered the influence of BC particle size on AAEBC by setting the lower and upper bounds at 0.8 and 1.2 (ref. 67), respectively.For the DC3, SEAC4RS, FIREX-AQ, GoAmazon and ORACLES campaigns, we used MAEBC,550 of 11.25 m2 g−1 and AAEBC of 1.0 as the BASE case. Beyond this base configuration, we examined the lower and upper bounds by varying MAEBC,550 (7.5 and 13.5 m2 g−1) and AAEBC (0.8 and 1.2), which generated eight additional combinations (Supplementary Table 2). These parameter combinations encompass the variabilities in BC mixing state, size distribution and coating sampled across different instruments and locations. The results from these eight combinations were used to represent the range of calculated BC and OC light absorption. Uncertainties in the derived OC absorption, propagated from instrumental measurement errors, were calculated via error propagation analysis (Supplementary Material 1), resulting in combined uncertainties of 31.6–36.1% (Supplementary Table 3).Using these derived absorption values, we can then calculate the two critical parameters that characterize the optical properties of OC: (1) the mass absorption efficiency of OC (MAEOC), which represents how efficiently each unit mass of OC absorbs light (units: m2 g−1), indicating the ‘darkness’ or light-absorbing strength per unit mass of material and (2) the absorption Ångström exponent of OC (AAEOC), which describes how rapidly the absorption efficiency changes with wavelength—a parameter that helps distinguish different types of light-absorbing particles, with values typically ranging from 1 (for BC) to 6–7 (for strongly wavelength-dependent BrC):$${\mathrm{MAE}}_{\mathrm{OC},\lambda }=\,{b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{OC}}\left(\lambda \right)/{\mathrm{Mass}}_{\mathrm{OC}}$$
                    (6)
                $${\mathrm{AAE}}_{\mathrm{OC}}=\mathrm{ln}({b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{OC}}\left({\lambda }_{1}\right)/{b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{OC}}\left({\lambda }_{2}\right))/\mathrm{ln}({\lambda }_{2}/{\lambda }_{1})$$
                    (7)
                where MassOC is the mass concentration of OC.Optical-only approachFor observational datasets from the AERONET network, Welgegund station and POLDER/GRASP satellite retrievals where aerosol mass concentration measurements were unavailable, we employed a method that independently separates BrC and BC absorption by using total absorption data at three wavelengths:$$\begin{array}{l}{b}_{\mathrm{abs}}({\rm{\lambda }})={b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{BC}}({\rm{\lambda }})+{b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{OC}}({\rm{\lambda }})={b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{BC}}({{\rm{\lambda }}}_{\mathrm{ref}})\times {({\rm{\lambda }}/{{\rm{\lambda }}}_{\mathrm{ref}})}^{-{\mathrm{AAE}}_{\mathrm{BC}}}\\ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,+{b}_{\mathrm{abs},\mathrm{OC}}({{\rm{\lambda }}}_{\mathrm{ref}})\times {({\rm{\lambda }}/{{\rm{\lambda }}}_{\mathrm{ref}})}^{-{\mathrm{AAE}}_{\mathrm{OC}}}\end{array}$$
                    (8)
                $$\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{AAOD}({\rm{\lambda }})={\mathrm{AAOD}}_{\mathrm{BC}}({\rm{\lambda }})+{\mathrm{AAOD}}_{\mathrm{OC}}({\rm{\lambda }})={\mathrm{AAOD}}_{\mathrm{BC}}({{\rm{\lambda }}}_{\mathrm{ref}})\\ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\times {\left({\rm{\lambda }}/{{\rm{\lambda }}}_{\mathrm{ref}}\right)}^{-{\mathrm{AAE}}_{\mathrm{BC}}}+\,{\mathrm{AAOD}}_{\mathrm{OC}}({{\rm{\lambda }}}_{\mathrm{ref}})\times {({\rm{\lambda }}/{{\rm{\lambda }}}_{\mathrm{ref}})}^{-{\mathrm{AAE}}_{\mathrm{OC}}}\end{array}$$
                    (9)
                where λ is the wavelength observed, λref is the reference wavelength we want to calculate; babs, BC(λref) and babs, OC(λref) are light absorption coefficients of BC and OC at λref, respectively; AAODBC(λref) and AAODOC(λref) are AAOD of BC and OC at λref, respectively.To maintain consistency throughout the study, the default AAEBC value was kept at 1.0 in equations (8) and (9). Additionally, we considered AAEBC values of 0.8 and 1.2 as the lower and upper bounds to determine the range of retrieved OC light absorption. In equation (8), AAEOC, babs, BC(λref) and babs, OC(λref) are three unknowns to be determined. Given the measured total absorption coefficients babs(λ) at any three wavelengths, we can establish a system of three equations with three unknowns, which can be solved to determine AAEOC, babs, BC(λref) and babs, OC(λref). Similarly, equation (9) can be solved using the same approach, where AAEOC, AAODBC(λref) and AAODOC(λref) are the three unknowns that can be determined from satellite measured AAOD(λ) at any three wavelengths.Following the same wavelength selection as in the aircraft observation analysis, we analysed AERONET and POLDER/GRASP satellite data at three reference wavelengths (404, 532 and 660 nm), which were retrieved from their respective observed wavelength data using equations (8) and (9). We tested the calculations with different combinations of observed wavelengths (λ) (for example, 443, 670 and 865 nm versus 490, 565 and 1,020 nm) for POLDER/GRASP satellite data, and the calculated AAEOC, AAODBC(λref) and AAODOC(λref) are close to each other (Supplementary Fig. 18).We validated our AAE separation methods through two independent approaches: comparison with field observations and inter-comparison among methods (Supplementary Section 4). These validations provide confidence in applying these methods globally to quantify d-BrC radiative effects.Model simulationsTo accurately estimate the impact of wildfire-derived BrC aerosols on solar shortwave radiation, we modified the optical properties of primary organic aerosol in the Community Earth System Model (CESM), with the coupled Community Atmosphere Model version 5.4 (CAM5.4) and Community Land Model version 4 (CLM4). The model employs the four-mode version of the Modal Aerosol Module (MAM4), which treats aerosol species of sulfate, mineral dust, sea salt, BC, primary organic aerosol (POA) and secondary organic aerosol in the Aitken, accumulation, coarse and primary carbon modes42. BC and POA are emitted into the primary carbon mode and subsequently transferred to the accumulation mode through ageing processes.Following Brown et al.28, we separated POA emissions into three distinct sources to enable targeted modification of biomass burning organic aerosol optical properties: biomass burning (BB) from wildfires using the Global Fire Emission Dataset Version 4.1 s (GFED4.1 s), biofuel (BF) combustion from residential and agricultural burning and fossil fuel (FF) combustion from anthropogenic energy and transport sectors. This source separation allows us to assign wavelength-dependent absorbing properties specifically to biomass burning POA (BB-POA) while maintaining default optical properties for other organic aerosol sources. We assigned wavelength-dependent absorption properties to BB-POA based on the MAEOC values derived from our observational analyses. The imaginary part of the complex refractive index (k) for BB-POA was calculated from the measured MAEOC values using the Mie theory, assuming spherical particles with a real refractive index of 1.65 and a particle density of 1.569 g cm−3 for BB-POA15,25. The relationship between MAEOC and k is given as:$${\mathrm{MAE}}_{\mathrm{OC}}\left(\lambda \right)=\frac{4\pi k(\lambda )}{\rho \lambda }$$
                    (10)
                where λ is wavelength, ρ is particle density. We derived k(λ) values at three key wavelengths (404 nm, 530 nm and 660 nm) corresponding to our observational data to capture the characteristic spectral dependence of BrC absorption. Because MAEOC values were derived from ambient atmospheric observations that already reflect the effects of photochemical ageing and atmospheric processing on the BrC absorption, we did not apply additional bleaching parameterizations in the model. This approach differs from previous studies that used laboratory-derived optical properties requiring subsequent ageing treatments.We conducted three sets of model experiments with the derived MAEOC values from our observational analyses (DC3, SEAC4RS, FIREX-AQ, GoAmazon and ORACLES campaigns) to explore the range of BrC radiative effects: (1) BRC_BASE with campaign-averaged MAEOC derived from MAEBC = 11.25 m2 g−1 and AAEBC = 1.0, representing the moderate BrC absorption with wavelength-independent characteristics; (2) BRC_MIN with MAEOC assigned as the minimum value derived from all nine parameter combinations (MAEBC = 7.5, 11.25, 13.5 m2 g−1 and AAEBC = 0.8, 1.0, 1.2) across all campaigns, representing the lower bound of BrC absorption; and (3) BRC_MAX with MAEOC assigned as the maximum value derived from all nine parameter combinations across all campaigns, representing the upper bound of BrC absorption. Each experiment was initialized with a 1-year spin-up period and conducted from 2012 to 2016 with prescribed monthly sea surface temperature and sea ice at a horizontal resolution of 0.9° latitude by 1.25° longitude with 30 vertical levels.The direct radiative effect (DRE) of an individual species (BC, BrC) was calculated as the difference between the total radiative flux at TOA and the flux without that specific species71, while keeping all other aerosol species unchanged. This approach isolates the contribution of BrC absorption to the Earth’s energy balance, allowing us to quantify the climatic impact of light-absorbing organic aerosols from biomass burning sources. Unlike previous BrC modelling studies that rely on optical properties derived from laboratory experiments or localized field observations23,24,27,28,29, our method utilizes BrC optical properties obtained from global ambient measurements across diverse atmospheric conditions. Furthermore, the derived range of BrC optical properties accounts for variations in particle mixing state and size distribution, providing a more representative characterization of real-world aerosol absorption.

Data availability
            
            The minimum datasets necessary to interpret, verify and extend the findings of this study are publicly available from the following repositories. DC3 and SEAC4RS aircraft campaign data are available from the NASA Airborne Science Data Archive (https://www-air.larc.nasa.gov/data.htm). FIREX-AQ campaign data are available from the NASA Atmospheric Science Data Center (ASDC) at https://www-air.larc.nasa.gov/missions/firex-aq/index.html (ref. 72). ORACLES campaign data are available from the NASA ESPO Data Archive at https://espoarchive.nasa.gov/archive/browse/oracles. GoAmazon observations are available from the US Department of Energy Atmospheric Radiation Measurement (ARM) Data Center (site MAO, 2014–2015) at https://adc.arm.gov/. Welgegund multi-wavelength aerosol absorption data are available from the Finnish Meteorological Institute repository under a CC-BY license at https://fmi.b2share.csc.fi/records/97baff28a7bd49459d6e5014e0279522. AERONET Version 3 aerosol optical depth and inversion products are publicly available at https://aeronet.gsfc.nasa.gov/. POLDER/GRASP aerosol products are publicly available at https://www.grasp-open.com/products/polder-data-release/. Global Fire Emissions Database (GFEDv4.1) data are available from the ORNL DAAC at https://doi.org/10.3334/ORNLDAAC/1293 (ref. 73). The model simulation results generated and analysed in this study are publicly available via figshare at https://doi.org/10.6084/m9.figshare.29446523.v2 (ref. 74). Access to certain NASA and DOE archives may require free registration. Source data are provided with this paper.
          Code availability
            
            The Community Earth System Model (CESM, version 1.2.2.1) used in this study is available from the National Center for Atmospheric Research (NCAR) under its community access policy at https://www.cesm.ucar.edu/. Access requires user registration and acceptance of NCAR’s licensing agreement.

items.title: Clima, agricoltura e cementificazione: così la Terra perde i suoi uccelli
items.description: Numerosi studi spiegano che i cambiamenti climatici, i pesticidi e l’artificializzazione dei suoli stanno decimando le popolazioni di uccelli.
items.body:
Proteggere le foreste di Posidonia dagli ancoraggi: ora c’è la legge, ma basta?
		
		
			La legge sulla valorizzazione del mare contiene una norma di principio per la tutela di un ecosistema unico, ma per ora non sono previsti strumenti reali. 
		
		
			 Biodiversità
			8 maggio 2026
			
				di Simone Santi

Come stanno gli ermellini in Italia, i fantasmi selvaggi delle Alpi
		
		
			Con le Olimpiadi invernali l’Italia ha perso una grande occasione per la conoscenza e la conservazione degli ermellini, di cui ci sono pochissimi dati. Ora c’è un progetto che vuole raccoglierli.
		
		
			 Biodiversità
			14 aprile 2026
			
				di Camilla Soldati

Perché, anche secondo la scienza, si deve porre fine all’allevamento di animali da pellicce
		
		
			L’Autorità europea per la sicurezza alimentare ha pubblicato il suo atteso parere scientifico sul benessere di visoni, volpi, cani procione e cincillà allevati per la produzione di pellicce.
		
		
			 Biodiversità
			16 marzo 2026
			
				di Joanna Swabe

Le piante selvatiche che curano il mondo (e che rischiamo di perdere)
			
			
				Alle piante medicinali e aromatiche, da cui dipendono i nostri sistemi alimentari, medicinali e culturali, è dedicata la Giornata mondiale della natura selvatica 2026.

Biodiversità

3 marzo 2026

Anche l’Italia si prepara ad accogliere la legge per il ripristino della natura
			
			
				In Parlamento è iniziato l’iter per il decreto legislativo che adotterà il regolamento europeo: le associazioni chiedono più finanziamenti per il piano.

Architettura

13 febbraio 2026

Sabbie contese: il fragile equilibrio tra turismo e conservazione ambientale
			
			
				Nel 2025 sono oltre 3.800 le tartarughe marine nate nelle spiagge del Salento. La tutela delle spiagge dove nidificano è contesa dai gestori dei lidi.

Biodiversità

31 ottobre 2025

Un futuro per gli elefanti: tutelarli con compassione in un mondo che cambia
			
			
				Oggi, 12 agosto, è la Giornata mondiale degli elefanti. Humane world for animals da oltre 25 anni si impegna nella tutela di uno dei mammiferi più grandi del nostro Pianeta, purtroppo a rischio di estinzione.

Biodiversità

12 agosto 2025

Abbiamo provato il Deebot X9: il robot aspirapolvere per chi ha animali in casa
			
			
				Pulizia automatica e smart, anche in case “vissute”: l’ultimo robot aspirapolvere e lavapavimenti è pensato per semplificare la vita quotidiana.

Biodiversità

3 luglio 2025

Perché continuano ad aprire maxiallevamenti, come quello di ovaiole ad Arborio
			
			
				In un’epoca in cui consapevolezze e dati non mancano gli allevamenti intensivi continuano ad aprire, come nel caso del maxiallevamento di galline ovaiole ad Arborio. A spese di ambiente, persone e animali.

Biodiversità

23 giugno 2025

items.title: Foreste, nelle Oasi WWF assorbite circa 100.000 tonnellate di CO2 ogni anno
items.description:
Con la Primavera delle Oasi, l’associazione riapre al pubblico le foreste presenti nelle sue aree protette e racconta la loro importanza
The post Fore
items.body: In evidenza WWF Italia: 60 anni per la natura In evidenza Il Panda Siamo Noi In evidenza L'orso bruno marsicano In evidenza Adotta una specie Alleate preziose della salute umana e della lotta all’inquinamento. Per questo, nella seconda parte del suo report “Effetto Oasi”, il WWF Italia si concentra sulle foreste. Il report, pubblicato a puntate in occasione della Primavera delle Oasi WWF, l’iniziativa nazionale che riapre al pubblico oltre 100 aree protette, racconta il ruolo centrale che le Oasi WWF svolgono da oltre mezzo secolo nella tutela di questi ecosistemi fragili. Dal forest bathing allo stoccaggio di CO2 Nello specifico le foreste svolgono dei servizi ecosistemici importantissimi, a partire dall’assorbimento della CO2.  In totale si stima che oltre 3 milioni di tonnellate di carbonio siano stoccate nelle Oasi WWF, con un assorbimento di circa 100.000 tonnellate di CO2 ogni anno. Questo ruolo prezioso viene giocato in particolar modo dalle foreste, che rappresentano quindi  un nostro fondamentale alleato nella lotta all’emergenza climatica, così come per la filtrazione e purificazione dell’acqua e il contrasto al dissesto idrogeologico. O servizi ancora più importanti in contesti antropizzati, dove i boschi urbani abbattono gli inquinanti atmosferici e riducono l’effetto delle isole di calore, contribuendo letteralmente a salvare migliaia di vite umane ogni anno. È questo il motivo per cui il WWF tutela, tramite le proprie Oasi urbane, decine di aree anche in contesti cittadini, in particolare in grandi città come Milano e Bologna. Infine, c’è un altro beneficio a cui possiamo accedere mettendo piede, con rispetto, nella magia di un bosco ben conservato: è quello psicologico e fisiologico, ormai sempre più documentato a livello scientifico, reso possibile dalle “immersioni in foresta”, meglio note come forest bathing, che il WWF ha cercato di promuovere rendendone possibile lo svolgimento, accompagnato da personale appositamente formato, in numerose Oasi da nord a sud del Paese. Anche perché le foreste sono state la nostra casa per decine di migliaia di anni, ed è fondamentale che si continui a considerarle tali. Il ruolo delle Oasi nella conservazione delle foreste Proprio per la loro importanza, il WWF è da decenni in prima linea negli sforzi per conservare le foreste, anche tramite la gestione diretta rappresentata dalle Oasi. Negli ‘80, grazie ad una simbolica vendita di francobolli,  è stata acquistata la grande foresta di Monte Arcosu in Sardegna, oltre 3.600 ettari dell’ancora più grande complesso forestale di Piscinamanna, tra le più intatte ed estese foreste mediterranee residue. Sempre in contesti mediterranei, lo stesso vale per il bosco planiziale di Policoro, scrigno di biodiversità genetica per le querce mediterranee. Tutelate sono anche alcune foreste costiere, come quelle retrodunali di Le Cesine, in Puglia,  di Dune Alberoni, in Veneto, sul versante adriatico, e di Macchiagrande e di Burano, sul versante tirrenico. Salendo di quota, sulle colline toscane si trova Bosco Rocconi, tra i più wild del WWF. Si arriva poi alle grandi foreste appenniniche, come la faggeta di Guardiaregia-Campochiaro, primo nucleo protetto dell’istituendo Parco Nazionale del Matese, o ai castagneti secolari di Montovolo e dei Ghirardi, nell’Appennino settentrionale. Spostandoci sempre più a nord, fondamentali tasselli di biodiversità sono quelli rappresentati dalle ultime foreste residue della Pianura Padana, quali il Bosco Vanzago e quello allagato di Valle Averto. Salendo di quota, si arriva poi alle Prealpi a Valpredina (Bergamo) e alle vere e proprie foreste alpine, purtroppo fortemente impattate a seguito della tempesta Vaia e patogeni susseguenti, con i boschi di Valtrigona, nelle Dolomiti del Lagorai (Trento). Dal lupo al cervo sardo, i successi nella tutela di specie simbolo e biodiversità In un Paese densamente abitato come l’Italia, la tutela di foreste in salute è stata determinante per consentire la sopravvivenza o il ritorno di grandi mammiferi e altre specie particolarmente vulnerabili. Le Oasi WWF agiscono come punti di connessione all’interno di paesaggi più ampi, favorendo gli spostamenti faunistici e la vitalità genetica delle popolazioni animali. Uno degli esempi più emblematici è rappresentato dall’Oasi WWF di Monte Arcosu. Quest’area ha rivestito un’importanza straordinaria per la conservazione del cervo sardo (Cervus elaphus corsicanus), sottospecie endemica della Sardegna e della Corsica che, nel corso del Novecento, ha rischiato l’estinzione a causa della caccia e della perdita di habitat. Grazie alla protezione garantita dall’Oasi, Monte Arcosu è divenuto il principale santuario della specie, ospitando una delle popolazioni più consistenti. Una storia simile arriva dall’Oasi WWF Gole del Sagittario, in Abruzzo. Questo territorio montano, caratterizzato da boschi, gole profonde e ambienti rupestri, rappresenta un’area di fondamentale importanza per l’espansione dell’orso bruno marsicano (Ursus arctos marsicanus), una delle sottospecie più rare al mondo, con una popolazione ridotta a rischio critico di estinzione. Più a nord, l’Oasi WWF dei Ghirardi, nell’Appennino parmense, ha offerto al lupo appenninico (Canis lupus italicus) condizioni favorevoli per il proprio ritorno e consolidamento, dopo decenni di persecuzione che ne avevano drasticamente ridotto la presenza. L’Oasi rappresenta oggi un territorio importante per la tutela di questa specie chiave, il cui recupero testimonia la capacità degli ecosistemi forestali protetti di ristabilire equilibri ecologici complessi, e che ha aiutato a contenere in maniera naturale la diffusione della peste suina africana in uno degli epicentri nazionali. Le Oasi forestali WWF, dunque, rappresentano un modello concreto di conservazione efficace, in cui tutela delle specie, salvaguardia degli habitat e benefici per le persone coincidono. L’esempio dall’ambientalismo alpino La tutela delle foreste è stata possibile grazie all’impegno e alla passione di esperti, volontari e attivisti. Tra le figure che hanno dato l’esempio c’è Francesco Borzaga, delegato regionale del WWF, fondatore e presidente del WWF Trentino – Alto Adige, scomparso nel marzo 2025. “La difesa dell’ambiente è faticosa”, ripeteva spesso. E lui quella fatica l’ha affrontata tutta, senza mai arretrare. Una storia, la sua, fatta di grandi battaglie: come quella per la Val di Genova, una delle ultime valli d’acque libere delle Alpi, incastonata nel Parco Naturale Adamello Brenta. Negli anni in cui si progettava la costruzione di quella che sarebbe potuta diventare la più grande centrale idroelettrica d’Europa, Borzaga comprese subito che la perdita di quell’ecosistema sarebbe stata irreversibile. Si oppose con determinazione e oggi, anche grazie al suo impegno, la Val di Genova è rimasta un luogo integro. È anche a Borzaga che si deve la nascita dell’Oasi WWF di Valtrigona, la prima Oasi alpina del WWF, situata nel cuore del Lagorai: la sua storia è un esempio di come l’amore per la natura, che dà significato ad una vita intera, possa continuare anche nel futuro. Gli appuntamenti del fine settimana della Primavera delle Oasi Il palinsesto della Primavera delle Oasi continua nel weekend del 15-17 maggio. Si parte il 14 maggio, quando l’Oasi WWF Le Cesine, in Salento, organizza un’esperienza di citizen science alla ricerca delle tracce del lupo, con un metodo di monitoraggio che consiste nel percorrere un percorso prestabilito alla ricerca di segni di presenza del predatore. Sempre il 14 maggio l’Oasi WWF le Foppe (Trezzo sull’Adda) festeggia i 35 anni di gestione con una visita notturna alla ricerca delle affascinanti lucciole. Il 15 maggio appuntamento a Melegnano per l’apertura dell’Oasi Urbana Parco delle Noci: un’occasione da non perdere per visitare questa particolare area verde che ricostruisce un bosco planiziale e le sue zone umide. Il 16 maggio all’Oasi di Serre-Persano (Salerno) si potrà partecipare ad un corso di fotografia naturalistica, oppure ad un bioblitz sulle farfalle all’Oasi di Valpredina (Bergamo), per finire con un aperitivo botanico sulle sponde della Laguna all’Oasi di Orbetello. Il 17 maggio invece si potrà andare alla scoperta del maestoso falco pellegrino nella selvaggia Oasi di Bosco Rocconi (Grosseto), scoprire il mondo delle api all’Oasi di Cà Brigida (Verucchio) o diventare “ranger per un giorno” all’Oasi di Ripa Bianca (Jesi). Ma sono tantissime altre le iniziative legate alla Primavera delle Oasi, che continueranno anche le prossime settimane fino al 2 giugno. Tutte le info su: www.wwf.it/cosa-facciamo/eventi/primavera-delle-oasi/ Effetto Oasi 2026 Le varie puntate del report WWF dedicato agli effetti delle Oasi e delle aree protette sulla nostra vita

items.title: Biomethane Academy, iscrizioni aperte
items.description: Sono aperte le iscrizioni per partecipare ai corsi della Biomethane Academy. Si tratta della prima scuola italiana interamente dedicata alla formazion
items.body:
Biomethane Academy, iscrizioni aperte

11 Maggio 2026

Sono aperte le iscrizioni per partecipare ai corsi della Biomethane Academy. Si tratta della prima scuola italiana interamente dedicata alla formazione professionale nel settore del biometano, nata dalla collaborazione tra CIB-Consorzio Italiano Biogas e Cornucopia Società Cooperativa Sociale ETS – ente formativo accreditato presso Regione Lombardia.L’iniziativa nasce per rispondere alle crescenti esigenze formative del nostro settore che, grazie anche ai fondi PNRR, sta vivendo una fase di sviluppo e forte espansione.I corsi prenderanno il via dal mese di ottobre 2026 e si articoleranno su due livelli, sulla base dei due nuovi profili professionali ufficialmente riconosciuti dal Quadro Regionale degli Standard Professionali (QRSP) della regione Lombardia:Operatore di impianto (150 ore) Pensato per chi è già in possesso delle principali abilitazioni, include teoria e pratica su impianti in funzione, con un focus operativo avanzato. I corsi relativi a questo profilo si svolgeranno in modalità ibrida: formazione a distanza, in presenza o in campo.Manager di impianti (130 ore) Il più alto livello formativo, curato dai migliori esperti del settore, con focus su management, normativa, sostenibilità e innovazione. I corsi relativi a questo profilo si svolgeranno in modalità formazione a distanza.I percorsi in modalità ibrida saranno integrati da visite didattiche in impianti di eccellenza, che consentiranno ai partecipanti di osservare sul campo processi e tecnologie all’avanguardia.I percorsi formativi rilasciano certificazioni riconosciute in tutta l’Unione Europea, a garanzia della spendibilità delle competenze acquisite sia in ambito nazionale che internazionale.Le aziende interessate a iscrivere i propri dipendenti ai corsi della Biomethane Academy potranno accedere a fondi regionali per la formazione continua. I tecnici di Cornucopia Società Cooperativa Sociale ETS affiancheranno le imprese in ogni fase del percorso, dalla presentazione della domanda fino alla rendicontazione, offrendo così un servizio completo e personalizzato.Per informazioni e pre-iscrizioni è possibile contattare ​​​​​​​formazione@consorziobiogas.it | Tel. +39 0371 4662633 int. 806 – 810 Biomethane Academy è un modello di formazione all’avanguardia con l’obiettivo di costruire un ecosistema di professionalità qualificate a supporto della filiera agricola e della sostenibilità ambientale. La scuola rappresenta un progetto innovativo in un momento chiave per la transizione ecologica del Paese, che formerà competenze tecniche altamente specializzate, aggiornate e certificate.A garanzia della qualità: il Comitato Tecnico ScientificoLe attività della Biomethane Academy sono supervisionate da un Comitato Tecnico Scientifico (CTS), che annovera figure di primo piano del mondo scientifico e industriale:Angelo Baronchelli, imprenditore e innovatore nel settore delle energie rinnovabili, fondatore di Gruppo AB, Vice presidente del CIB;Valeria Comensoli, imprenditrice agricola bresciana dell’Azienda Agricola Malgherosse;Matteo Grandi, Country Manager di Verdalia Bioenergy in Italia;Federico Frascari, direttore tecnico di Biological Care, biologo esperto in gestione di impianti di biometanoAndrea Chiabrando, direttore tecnico del Consorzio Monviso Agroenergia, esperto di modelli integrati di economia circolare in agricoltura						
				
				
				
					
			
						
									VISITA IL SITO DI BIOMETHANE ACADEMY

items.title: Global patterns of urban heat shaped by climate and morphology
items.body:
Download PDF

Article
    
        
            Open access

Published: 11 May 2026

Global patterns of urban heat shaped by climate and morphology
                    Siwoo Lee1 na1, Cheolhee Yoo 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-3922-23002,3 na1, Bokyung Son 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-8410-731X1, Dongjin Cho4, Jungho Im 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-4506-68771,5,6 & …T. C. Chakraborty 
            ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0003-1338-35257 Show authors

Nature Communications

(2026) Cite this article
                    
                    
                    
                            We are providing an unedited version of this manuscript to give early access to its
                            findings. Before final publication, the manuscript will undergo further editing. Please note
                            there may be errors present which affect the content, and all legal disclaimers apply.

Subjects
        
            AttributionClimate-change impactsProjection and prediction

AbstractUrban morphology and background climate are evolving drivers of the urban heat island (UHI) effect, yet the thermal influence of surrounding urban structure and its coupling with climate remain elusive. Here, we show that climate and morphology jointly shape global urban heat. Using a six-class urban typology, long-term climatology, and machine learning, we quantify the thermal influence of surrounding urban structure and project future urban heat across 2,213 cities. We define a city-level thermal impact of the surrounding built environment (TBE) as the area-weighting UHI change induced by specific built-up types. Climatically, cold regions most frequently exhibit high daytime TBE, while arid regions dominate high nighttime TBE. Structurally, a universal pattern persists; high TBE corresponds to denser and taller forms, whereas sparser and lower types dominate low TBE during day and night. Future projections indicate that climate change dominates TBE change in 69% of cities, whereas the Global South exhibits stronger tendencies toward morphology-driven and synergistic intensification than the Global North. Our results highlight the need for locally tailored adaptation strategies that target the dominant drivers—climatic, morphological, or both.

items.title: Omaggio a Sir David Attenborough
items.description: David Attenborough ha compiuto 100 anni l’8 maggio 2026: un traguardo che non riguarda solo la sua straordinaria longevità, ma anche l’impatto che ha
items.body:

                                                Omaggio a Sir David Attenborough

Omaggio a Sir David Attenborough

Tag:
                                                                            Informazione ambientale                                                                    
                                                    
                                    
            
            
            
                
                                            Pubblicato il:
                        
                            8 Maggio 2026

Condividi

Ha compiuto 100 anni il divulgatore naturalistico David Attenborough che ha insegnato a milioni di persone a guardare al mondo come un ecosistema complesso dove la sfera biologica e quella inorganica condividono un destino comune

David Attenborough ha compiuto 100 anni l’8 maggio 2026: un traguardo che non riguarda solo la sua straordinaria longevità, ma anche l’impatto che ha avuto nel portare la scienza, la natura e la crisi climatica al grande pubblico. Sir David Attenborough è stato il pioniere britannico della divulgazione naturalistica: per decenni, con i suoi documentari è stato fonte di ispirazione per molte generazioni di appassionati di tutto il mondo.  Naturalista e voce inconfondibile della natura per la BBC, Sir David Attenborough, vanta una carriera che dura da oltre settanta anni. Laureato in Scienze Naturali a Cambridge, nel 1976, iniziò la produzione di Vita sulla Terra. Fu la prima serie naturalistica di questo genere che rivoluzionò il modo di raccontare la vita sulla Terra. Il viaggio triennale intorno al mondo, comprese la visita di 40 paesi e le riprese di 600 specie. Seguirono poi le serie come Life on Earth, The Private Life of Plants e The Blue Planet, e nel 2025 vinse il Daytime Emmy Award come “Personalità dell’anno per un programma non quotidiano”, diventando così il più anziano vincitore nella storia del prestigioso premio televisivo.  Un anno fa, nel 2025 ha presentato Ocean, un documentario di lungometraggio definito da lui stesso come “uno dei progetti più importanti della sua carriera“, con l’obiettivo dichiarato di contribuire alla salvaguardia della biodiversità e al contrasto del cambiamento climatico.  Il documentarista che dopo aver trascorso una vita intera a filmare la terra, ha commentato che il luogo più importante non è sulla terra ma nel mare:  “se salviamo il mare, salviamo il nostro mondo”.
Negli ultimi anni, la sua voce si è fatta più urgente. Gli ultimi documentari come A Life on Our Planet o Extinction: The Facts affrontano apertamente i disastri ambientali causati dall’uomo. Attenborough riesce ancora a farsi ascoltare perché parla di soluzioni, non solo di problemi.  Tra i suoi interventi più memorabili c’è quello alla COP26 di Glasgow, in Scozia nel 2021, quando parlò ai leader mondiali con un appello netto: la crisi climatica non era più un problema lontano, ma una sfida presente, urgente e collettiva. In quell’occasione ricordò che siamo già nei guai e che la via d’uscita passava da un’azione comune, rapida e concreta per fermare le emissioni e proteggere il futuro. Nel suo intervento Attenborough sottolineò alcuni punti chiave: la stabilità climatica che ha permesso lo sviluppo della civiltà umana, la necessità di ridurre rapidamente le emissioni di carbonio e l’urgenza di mantenere vivo l’obiettivo di evitare l’aumento della temperatura di 1,5 °C. Il suo discorso era insieme scientifico ed etico: non chiedeva solo politiche migliori, ma una visione condivisa del destino umano.  Una delle frasi più forti del suo messaggio fu che, se divisi siamo stati abbastanza potenti da danneggiare il pianeta, uniti possiamo essere abbastanza forti da salvarlo. È un passaggio cruciale perché trasforma il cambiamento climatico da questione tecnica a scelta di civiltà, chiamando in causa governi, imprese e cittadini.  “Ricordare Attenborough è importante perché il suo messaggio riesce ancora oggi a parlare a tutte le generazioni in modo trasversale. Il suo valore sta nell’aver reso accessibile un tema complesso senza semplificarlo, unendo dati e capacità narrativa in un linguaggio comprensibile ed aver insegnato a guardare al mondo come un ecosistema complesso che accomuna in un unico destino sfera biologica e sfera inorganica”, commenta Pietro Rubellini, direttore generale di ARPAT.  “È ancora oggi una fonte di ispirazione perché mostra come l’autorevolezza e la divulgazione possono essere utilizzate per coinvolgere e responsabilizzare un pubblico ampio – prosegue Rubellini -.  A cento anni, la sua voce continua a ricordare che la difesa della natura non è un gesto simbolico, ma un patto concreto di solidarietà tra generazioni”.
E il valore più grande del suo intervento alla COP26 è l’aver esplicitato ai leader del mondo che il tempo dell’attesa è finito, ma che una soluzione è ancora possibile se l’umanità agisce insieme. Festeggiare i suoi 100 anni significa anche rinnovare l’impegno che ha chiesto al mondo: proteggere il clima, difendere la biodiversità e lasciare ai più giovani non solo parole, ma un pianeta ancora vivibile.  Il discorso pronunciato da Sir David Attenborough alla COP26 di Glasgow il 1° novembre 2021 rimane uno dei momenti più iconici della diplomazia climatica moderna. In qualità di portavoce dei cittadini, Attenborough si rivolse ai leader mondiali con un appello che univa rigore scientifico e un profondo senso di urgenza morale.
Di seguito, riportiamo alcuni dei passaggi salienti del suo appello finale in Scozia, in cui richiama tutti all’azione: “Dopotutto, siamo i più grandi risolutori di problemi che siano mai esistiti sulla Terra. Ora capiamo questo problema. …. Dobbiamo fermare le emissioni di carbonio in questo decennio. Dobbiamo recuperare miliardi di tonnellate di carbonio dall’aria. Una nuova rivoluzione industriale, alimentata da milioni di innovazioni sostenibili, è essenziale ed è già iniziata”… “Condivideremo tutti i benefici: energia pulita e accessibile, aria sana e cibo a sufficienza per sostenerci tutti. La natura è un alleato fondamentale. Ogni volta che ripristineremo la natura, essa recupererà il carbonio e ci aiuterà a riportare l’equilibrio sul nostro pianeta. Se divisi siamo stati abbastanza potenti da destabilizzare il nostro pianeta, sicuramente, lavorando insieme siamo abbastanza potenti da salvarlo” – e conclude – “Nella mia vita, ho assistito a un terribile declino. Nel vostro, potreste e dovreste assistere a una meravigliosa guarigione. Quella speranza disperata, signore e signori, delegati, eccellenze, è il motivo per cui il mondo sta guardando a voi e perché voi siete qui”.
Questo discorso è considerato una pietra miliare perché sposta il focus dalla pura contabilità delle emissioni a una questione di responsabilità intergenerazionale. Attenborough ha sottolineato che, sebbene la nostra specie sia stata capace di causare danni senza precedenti, possiede anche le competenze e l’ingegno per invertire la rotta. La sua visione funge da promemoria costante per i decisori politici: la lotta al cambiamento climatico non è un’opzione tra le tante, ma una necessità per garantire la sopravvivenza della civiltà umana e la protezione della biosfera. La testimonianza di David Attenborough non è solo un racconto del cambiamento climatico ma un invito potente a sperare e agire: ci ricorda che possiamo davvero cambiare il mondo e ci offre una guida su come farlo: proteggere la biodiversità, riforestare, abbracciare le energie rinnovabili e ripensare le nostre abitudini. È un messaggio di speranza e responsabilità che può ispirare ogni persona che viva su questo pianeta.
Foto di Pexels da Pixabay

items.title: Lo stato delle acque dei fiumi Arno, Sieve e alcuni loro affluenti nel 2024

Lo stato delle acque dei fiumi Arno, Sieve e alcuni loro affluenti nel 2024
